narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream of becoming a lawyer, and we appreciate the strengths in your profile, like your solid undergraduate GPA of 3.20 and LSAT score of 35, which are right around what many applicants bring to the table. However, the admissions model we use to predict first-time bar exam success has assessed your overall profile and estimated an 82% chance that you would fail the bar on your first try, which is why your application was rejected—our goal is to admit students with the highest predicted odds of passing to ensure their long-term success. The biggest factors pulling the prediction toward failure were your Year 1 and Year 3 school decile rankings, both at the 1st decile, meaning your grades in those early years placed you at the lower end compared to your peers, and this stood out as the most influential concern for the model. Your undergraduate GPA and LSAT, while close to averages, helped nudge things slightly in a positive direction by suggesting some academic capability. Being in the middle family income bracket had a very minor push toward failure, while your plan to attend full-time worked a bit in your favor, as that's the norm for most successful students. Overall, your early low deciles were far below typical applicants' mid-range standings, which overshadowed your other solid metrics and drove the high failure risk. This doesn't define your potential—many students overcome early hurdles. To strengthen future applications, focus on boosting your academic deciles through stronger coursework or targeted prep, perhaps retaking the LSAT for a higher score, and gathering letters highlighting your resilience as a Black woman in this field. Consider community college or bridge programs to rebuild your record. Reapply after addressing these gaps; persistence pays off. We're rooting for you.
